machineTranslated.title: Machine-translated page
machineTranslated.text: This page was translated automatically, so it may contain mistakes or read a little oddly. The English version is the source of truth.

meta.title: verbatra: incremental i18n translation for locale files
meta.description: Keep your i18n locale files in sync. verbatra translates only the keys that changed, through your choice of AI or machine-translation provider. Start now.
meta.ogDescription: Keep i18n locale files in sync. Translate only changed keys.
meta.ogImageAlt: verbatra keeps your i18n locale files in sync and translates only the keys that changed.
meta.definition: verbatra is a CLI and SDK that keeps your i18n locale files in sync, translating only the keys that changed through your choice of AI or machine-translation provider.

hero.headline: Translate only what changed.
hero.lead: verbatra is a CLI and SDK that keeps your i18n locale files in sync. You maintain the source locale; it fills every other locale through the AI provider you choose.

pillars.heading: One tool for the whole translation loop.
pillars.lead: verbatra is more than AI translation. It covers machine translation you control, a clean Excel handoff for human translators, and the automation to keep every locale in sync.

ai.title: AI translation
ai.body: Translate only what changed through your choice of provider: Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepL, or any OpenAI-compatible server you run yourself. A committed lock file keeps every run incremental, and placeholder and ICU integrity is checked before anything is written.

excel.title: Excel handoff
excel.body: Run verbatra export to produce a styled .xlsx workbook, hand it to a translation office or in-house translators, then verbatra import reads their edits back into your locale files. Mix machine translation with professional human work.

automation.title: Automation
automation.body: Keep every locale in sync as you work: verbatra watch translates on change, CI-friendly exit codes and --json output slot into any pipeline, and a GitHub Action runs it in your workflow.

status.label: Build status, coverage, package versions, downloads, and license

compat.heading: One tool, every framework.
compat.body: verbatra reads the locale files your stack already uses: JSON for i18next, vue-i18n, next-intl, and ngx-translate, plus XLIFF, YAML, and Flutter ARB. It fills them through the provider you choose, no new format to adopt.

providers.heading: Translate through the model you trust.
providers.lead: Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepL, or any OpenAI-compatible server you host yourself. Pick one in a single line of config and switch providers without touching your locale files.

how.heading: A run is a short, predictable pipeline.
how.lead: No magic, no background sync: a deterministic sequence you can read top to bottom.

configure.title: Configure
configure.body: Point verbatra at your source locale, list the target languages, and pick a provider. Configuration lives in one file; API keys stay in your environment.

diff.title: Diff
diff.body: Each run compares your source locale against the committed lock file and sorts every key into new, changed, unchanged, or orphaned.

translate.title: Translate
translate.body: Only the new and changed keys are sent to your provider, in batches, with your glossary and tone applied. Everything else is left untouched.

verifyWrite.title: Verify & write
verifyWrite.body: Placeholder and ICU integrity are checked on every result. Anything that breaks is withheld; the rest is written to your locale files and the lock is updated.

why.heading: Built for teams that ship in many languages.
why.lead: Every choice favors predictability: small diffs, explicit config, and nothing written that was not verified first.

incremental.title: Incremental by default
incremental.body: A committed lock file records what was translated. Each run diffs the source and calls the provider only for the keys that changed.

provider.title: Your choice of provider
provider.body: Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepL, or a local OpenAI-compatible model, chosen in one line of config. Switch providers without touching your locale files.

safety.title: Safe by construction
safety.body: Placeholder and ICU integrity are checked after every translation. A result that breaks a placeholder is withheld, never written.

environment.title: Keys stay in your environment
environment.body: Provider API keys are read from environment variables, never from the config file, never from CLI arguments, and never logged.

oneEngine.title: CLI and SDK, one engine
oneEngine.body: The verbatra command and @verbatra/sdk share a single orchestration core, so scripts, CI, and your own tooling behave identically.

dryRun.title: Dry runs and watch mode
dryRun.body: Preview exactly what a run would change without writing anything, or keep a watch running that translates as your source locale evolves.

faq.heading: Frequently asked questions
faq.supporting: Short, direct answers to the questions teams ask before adopting verbatra.

incremental.question: How does verbatra avoid re-translating everything on every run?
incremental.answer: It keeps a committed lock file that records what was already translated. Each run diffs your source locale against that lock and sends only the new or changed keys to your provider. Unchanged keys are never touched, so a typical run costs a handful of strings, not your whole catalog.

providers.question: Which translation providers can I use?
providers.answer: Five, behind one interface: Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gemini (LLMs), DeepL (machine translation), and openai-compatible, which points verbatra at a local or self-hosted server like Ollama, LM Studio, or vLLM. You pick one by id in your config and can switch later by editing that one line. Gemini has a real free tier, and a local model costs nothing per call.

formats.question: Which formats and frameworks does verbatra support?
formats.answer: JSON for i18next, vue-i18n, next-intl, and ngx-translate, plus XLIFF, YAML, and Flutter ARB. That covers React, Next.js, Vue, Nuxt, Angular, SvelteKit, Astro, Node.js, and Flutter. verbatra reads the files you already have; there is no verbatra-specific format to migrate to.

studio.question: What is Verbatra Studio?
studio.answer: A local web dashboard over your project that shows every locale's status live, surfaces a review queue of flagged entries, and lets you fix translations in place. Start it with verbatra studio (install @verbatra/studio first). Editing your own locale files works out of the box; calling a provider from the UI is opt-in behind --allow-spend. It runs on 127.0.0.1 only and never leaves your machine.

safety.question: What stops a bad translation from breaking my app?
safety.answer: Every result passes a placeholder and ICU integrity check before it is written. A translation that drops, invents, or reorders a placeholder, or produces invalid ICU message syntax, is withheld rather than saved to your locale file. The same gate applies to edits and retranslations made in Verbatra Studio.

keys.question: Where do my API keys live, and is my content sent anywhere I do not control?
keys.answer: Keys are read only from environment variables (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY, GEMINI_API_KEY, DEEPL_API_KEY), never from the config file, CLI arguments, or logs. Only the strings you are translating are sent, and only to the provider you chose. If you use the openai-compatible provider with a local model, nothing leaves your machine or network at all.

preview.question: Can I preview a run before it writes anything?
preview.answer: Yes. A dry run reports exactly which keys would be sent and written without touching your files, and verbatra check and verbatra diff report per-locale drift without calling a provider. Watch mode does the opposite: it keeps translating as your source locale changes.

automation.question: Can I use verbatra in scripts, CI, and GitHub Actions?
automation.answer: Yes. The CLI is a thin wrapper over @verbatra/sdk, which exposes the same translate, watch, check, and diff operations for your own tooling. Commands return CI-friendly exit codes and support --json output, and an official GitHub Action runs verbatra in your workflow.

cost.question: What does verbatra cost?
cost.answer: verbatra itself is free and open source under the MIT license. The only cost is your provider's usage, which you control: it translates only what changed, and you can run it for free on Gemini's free tier or a local OpenAI-compatible model.

production.question: Is verbatra production-ready?
production.answer: It is young and actively developed, so pin a version and read the changelog when you upgrade. The safety model is already solid: every run diffs against a committed lock file, checks placeholder and ICU integrity on each result, and withholds anything that breaks rather than writing it. Teams run it in CI today; pin versions and you are on stable ground.

finalClose.heading: Stop hand-syncing locale files.
finalClose.lead: Add verbatra, commit the lock file, and let every run touch only what changed.

footer.tagline: Translate only what changed. An open-source CLI and SDK that keeps your i18n locale files in sync through the provider you choose.
footer.legalLine: Open-source under the MIT License · © 2026 Mario Kreitz

meta.title: Privacy policy
meta.description: How the verbatra documentation site handles your data.

privacy.title: Privacy policy
privacy.disclaimer: This is a translation for convenience. In case of any discrepancy, the English version prevails.

s1.heading: 1. Responsible party
s1.body: The party responsible for data processing on this site is Mario Kreitz, Germany. You can reach us by email at <email>mario.kreitz@web.de</email>.

s2.heading: 2. Overview
s2.body: This site is built to respect your privacy. It sets no cookies, runs no cross-site tracking, and collects only the minimal data needed to operate the site and understand aggregate usage. The sections below explain exactly what is processed and why.

s3.heading: 3. Server logs and hosting
s3.body: The site is self-hosted on a virtual server (managed with Dokploy). When you visit, the server processes standard access logs that may include your IP address, the date and time of the request, the requested URL, the referring page, and your browser's user-agent string. This data is used to operate and secure the site, for example to deliver pages, diagnose errors, and defend against abuse. The legal basis is Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR (our legitimate interest in a secure, functioning service). These logs are retained only for a short period.

s4.heading: 4. Analytics
s4.body: We use Umami, a privacy-friendly analytics tool that we self-host. Umami is cookieless and does not collect personal data or use cross-site fingerprinting. It records only aggregated metrics such as page views, referrers, approximate country, and browser, operating system, and device type. The legal basis is Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR (our legitimate interest in understanding how the site is used). You can read more about Umami in its documentation.

s5.heading: 5. Cookies
s5.body: This site sets no cookies. Because nothing requiring consent is stored on your device, no cookie consent banner is required.

s6.heading: 6. Fonts and assets
s6.body: Fonts are self-hosted: they are bundled at build time, so no requests are made to Google Fonts or any other third party when you load a page. The one exception is status badges: the home page and the testing page embed live badge images from GitHub, Codecov, and shields.io that show the current build, coverage, version, and license. Loading these images transmits your IP address to those providers, which act as independent controllers under their own privacy policies. The legal basis is Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR (our legitimate interest in showing accurate project status). Apart from these badges and the self-hosted Umami script described above, no third-party scripts or embeds load at runtime.

s7.heading: 7. GitHub
s7.body: The project's source code, issues, and discussions are hosted on <repo>GitHub</repo>. If you choose to interact there (for example, by opening an issue or joining a discussion), GitHub processes your data as an independent controller and its <ghprivacy>privacy statement</ghprivacy> applies.

s8.heading: 8. Your rights
s8.body: Under the GDPR you have the rights of access, rectification, erasure, restriction of processing, objection, and data portability (Art. 15–21 GDPR). You also have the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority. To exercise any of these rights, contact us using the details below.

s9.heading: 9. Contact
s9.body: For any privacy-related questions or requests, email <email>mario.kreitz@web.de</email>.

s10.heading: 10. Changes to this policy
s10.body: We may update this privacy policy as the site evolves or as legal requirements change. The date below reflects the most recent revision.

meta.title: Imprint
meta.description: Legal disclosure (Impressum) for the verbatra documentation site.

imprint.intro: This legal notice (Impressum) is provided in German as required by German law.
